what: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: R package accessing data published Office Budget Responsibility (OBR).

title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: Office Budget Responsibility UK’s independent fiscal watchdog. created 2010 coalition government provide independent check government’s fiscal plans - role previously held HM Treasury . distinction matters. HM Treasury government department sets fiscal policy: decides tax rates, spending plans, much government intends borrow. OBR’s job scrutinise plans independently, producing economic fiscal forecasts influenced ministers. Think equivalent Bank England’s independence monetary policy, applied public finances. practice, means OBR publishes forecasts Budget Autumn Statement showing whether thinks government track meet fiscal rules - political incentive optimistic.

title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
code: install.packages("obr")  # Or install the development version from GitHub # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("charlescoverdale/obr")

title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: package covers five datasets listed .

title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: OBR data freely available obr.uk. problem available: Excel files non-standard layouts, inconsistent headers, footnote-laden sheets require significant wrangling usable R. example, Public Finances Databank column headers buried row 4 spreadsheet, data starting row 8, trailing footnote numbers appended column names. Historical Forecasts Database stores forecasts vintage matrix - rows fiscal events, columns fiscal years - needs reshaping long format can plotted analysed. package handles automatically. One function call returns clean, tidy data frame. Data cached locally subsequent calls instant.
code: # Without this package path <- "~/Downloads/Public_finances_databank_March_2025.xlsx" raw  <- readxl::read_excel(path, sheet = "Aggregates (£bn)", col_names = FALSE) series_names <- as.character(unlist(raw[4, ])) # ... 30 more lines of wrangling ...  # With this package library(obr) get_psnb()

title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: download functions accept refresh = TRUE force fresh download OBR website.

what: 1. How much did COVID cost the UK?
title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: UK borrowed £318bn 2020-21 - roughly seven times pre-pandemic level - fund furlough, bounce-back loans, emergency NHS spending.

what: 2. How has the OBR’s borrowing forecast changed over time?
title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: OBR first forecast 2024-25 borrowing £37bn (March 2022). November 2025, estimate risen £149bn - four times original figure. get_forecasts() function returns every published forecast across fiscal events, making straightforward visualise forecast drift assess fiscal plans evolved.
code: psnb_fc <- get_forecasts("PSNB") fc_2425 <- psnb_fc[psnb_fc$fiscal_year == "2024-25", c("forecast_date", "value")] fc_2425 #>    forecast_date   value #>     March 2022    36.5 #>  November 2022    84.3 #>     March 2023    85.4 #>  November 2023    84.6 #>     March 2024    87.2 #>   October 2024   127.5 #>     March 2025   137.3 #>  November 2025   149.5

what: 3. Has the UK ever run a surplus - and how likely is it to again?
title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: UK last ran surplus 2000-01. 24 years since, government borrowed every year. Combine get_forecasts("PSNB_pct") see whether OBR projects future surpluses.

what: 4. Where does government revenue come from?
title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: Income tax, VAT, National Insurance together account around 60% government receipts. Breaking time reveals long-run shifts - rising share income tax fiscal drag pulls earners higher bands.
code: receipts <- get_receipts()  # Top tax sources in 2023-24 r <- receipts[receipts$year == "2023-24", ] r <- r[order(-r$value), ] head(r[, c("series", "value")], 8) #>                              series   value #>   Public sector current receipts   1101.5 #>                       Income tax    290.4 #>                              VAT    183.1 #>    National insurance contributions 182.4 #>                    Corporation tax   88.4 #>                       Council tax   44.9 #>                         Fuel duty   24.5 #>                    Stamp duties     18.4

what: 5. What does the March 2026 Budget forecast for borrowing?
title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: EFO detailed tables also include full breakdown: current receipts, current expenditure, depreciation, net investment, net borrowing - enabling see exactly deficit projected narrow.
code: efo <- get_efo_fiscal() efo[efo$series == "Net borrowing", ] #>   fiscal_year        series value_bn #>       2025-26 Net borrowing    132.7 #>       2026-27 Net borrowing    115.5 #>       2027-28 Net borrowing     96.5 #>       2028-29 Net borrowing     86.0 #>       2029-30 Net borrowing     63.4 #>       2030-31 Net borrowing     59.0

what: 6. Is the UK’s incapacity benefits bill rising?
title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: Incapacity benefit spending caseloads risen sharply since pandemic - key driver welfare reform debate 2025.
code: welfare <- get_welfare_spending() # Working-age incapacity spending, last 10 years ic <- welfare[welfare$series == "Working-age incapacity benefits spending" &               welfare$year >= "2014-15", ] ic #>        year                                      series value #>     2014-15 Working-age incapacity benefits spending  1.44 #>     2015-16 Working-age incapacity benefits spending  1.33 #>     ... #>     2023-24 Working-age incapacity benefits spending  1.78 #>     2024-25 Working-age incapacity benefits spending  2.02 #>     2025-26 Working-age incapacity benefits spending  2.16  # Number of people on incapacity benefits cases <- get_incapacity_caseloads() cases[cases$series == "Share of working age population", ] #>  2008-09   Share of working age population  6.80 #>  ... #>  2023-24   Share of working age population  6.82

what: 7. What happens to the state pension bill as the UK ages?
title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: OBR’s central projection state pension rising 4.6% GDP today 7.7% 2073-74 UK population ages. FSR also publishes scenarios higher/lower life expectancy different triple-lock uprating assumptions.
code: proj <- get_pension_projections()  # Central demographic projection: pension spending rises from 5% to 7.7% of GDP central <- proj[proj$scenario_type == "Demographic scenarios" &                 proj$scenario == "Central projection", ] head(central[, c("fiscal_year", "pct_gdp")], 5) #>   fiscal_year pct_gdp #>       2023-24    4.56 #>       2024-25    4.95 #>       2025-26    5.06 #>       2026-27    5.13 #>       2027-28    5.05  tail(central[, c("fiscal_year", "pct_gdp")], 5) #>   fiscal_year pct_gdp #>       2069-70    7.73 #>       2070-71    7.82 #>       2071-72    7.77 #>       2072-73    7.66 #>       2073-74    7.65

title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: package part family R packages UK economic fiscal data. share consistent interface - named functions, tidy data frames, local caching - designed work together. outputs join cleanly date columns.

title: Access Office for Budget Responsibility Data
text: two core datasets - Public Finances Databank Historical Official Forecasts Database - accessed via stable generic URLs OBR keeps pointed latest file. update automatically whenever call refresh = TRUE. EFO, WTR, FSR functions work differently. OBR publishes edition new URL containing publication date (e.g. march-2026-economic--fiscal-outlook-...), maintain generic redirect. means URLs package hardcoded specific edition: calls get_efo_fiscal() always return March 2026 Budget forecasts package updated, even OBR publishes new edition. OBR publishes roughly predictable schedule - EFO twice year (March October/November), FSR WTR year . package updated track new edition, meaning 2–3 patch releases per year. Check NEWS see edition function currently reflects. need data absolute latest publication package update available, download directly obr.uk.

title: Clear cached OBR files — clear_cache
text: Deletes files downloaded cached obr package. next function call re-download fresh data OBR website.

Clear cached OBR files — clear_cache
text: Invisibly returns NULL.

title: Clear cached OBR files — clear_cache
Get EFO economy projections — get_efo_economy
text: Downloads (caches) OBR Economic Fiscal Outlook Detailed Forecast Tables — Economy file returns quarterly economic projections chosen measure tidy long format.

title: Get EFO economy projections — get_efo_economy
Get EFO economy projections — get_efo_economy
text: measure Character. economy table return. One "labour", "inflation", "output_gap". Defaults "inflation". refresh Logical. TRUE, re-download even cached copy exists. Defaults FALSE.

title: Get EFO economy projections — get_efo_economy
text: data frame columns: period Calendar quarter, e.g. "2025Q1" (character) series Variable name, e.g. "CPI" (character) value Value units appropriate series (numeric)

title: Get EFO economy projections — get_efo_economy
text: Data runs 2008 Q1 current forecast horizon (OBR, March 2026). Use list_efo_economy_measures see available measures.

title: Get EFO economy projections — get_efo_economy

title: Get EFO fiscal projections (net borrowing components) — get_efo_fiscal
text: Downloads (caches) OBR Economic Fiscal Outlook Detailed Forecast Tables — Aggregates file returns components net borrowing (Table 6.5) tidy long format.

title: Get EFO fiscal projections (net borrowing components) — get_efo_fiscal
Get EFO fiscal projections (net borrowing components) — get_efo_fiscal
text: refresh Logical. TRUE, re-download even cached copy exists. Defaults FALSE.

title: Get EFO fiscal projections (net borrowing components) — get_efo_fiscal
text: data frame columns: fiscal_year Fiscal year forecast, e.g. "2025-26" (character) series Component name, e.g. "Net borrowing" (character) value_bn Projected value £ billion (numeric)

title: Get EFO fiscal projections (net borrowing components) — get_efo_fiscal
text: Covers five-year forecast horizon published recent Budget (OBR, March 2026). Key series include current receipts, current expenditure, depreciation, net investment, net borrowing (PSNB).

title: Get EFO fiscal projections (net borrowing components) — get_efo_fiscal
code: # \donttest{ efo <- get_efo_fiscal() #> ℹ Downloading efo_aggregates.xlsx from OBR... #> ✔ Saved to cache. efo[efo$series == "Net borrowing", ] #>    fiscal_year        series  value_bn #> 43     2025-26 Net borrowing 132.73508 #> 44     2026-27 Net borrowing 115.46142 #> 45     2027-28 Net borrowing  96.46737 #> 46     2028-29 Net borrowing  86.01563 #> 47     2029-30 Net borrowing  63.40344 #> 48     2030-31 Net borrowing  59.01991 # }

text: Downloads (caches) OBR Public Finances Databank returns annual Total Managed Expenditure (TME) £ billion. TME broadest measure UK government spending.

what: Get OBR forecast history for a fiscal series — get_forecasts
title: Get OBR forecast history for a fiscal series — get_forecasts
text: Downloads (caches) OBR Historical Official Forecasts Database returns tidy long-format data frame showing every forecast OBR ever published given series. row one forecast one fiscal year, made one fiscal event.

title: Get OBR forecast history for a fiscal series — get_forecasts
Get OBR forecast history for a fiscal series — get_forecasts
text: series Character. series return. Use list_forecast_series see options. Defaults "PSNB" (Public Sector Net Borrowing £bn). refresh Logical. TRUE, re-download even cached copy exists. Defaults FALSE.

title: Get OBR forecast history for a fiscal series — get_forecasts
text: data frame columns: series Series name supplied (character) forecast_date forecast published, e.g. "March 2024" (character) fiscal_year fiscal year forecast, e.g. "2024-25" (character) value Forecast value (numeric)

title: Get OBR forecast history for a fiscal series — get_forecasts
text: useful visualising OBR forecasts evolved time, comparing forecasts outturns.

title: Get OBR forecast history for a fiscal series — get_forecasts

Use `refresh = TRUE` to re-download. unique(ib$series) #> [1] "Employment and support allowance (excluding assessment phase)" #> [2] "Incapacity benefit"                                            #> [3] "Invalidity benefit"                                            #> [4] "Sickness benefit"                                              #> [5] "Severe disablement allowance"                                  #> [6] "Universal credit health (excluding assessment phase)"          #> [7] "Income support (incapacity/sick and disabled)"                 #> [8] "Incapacity benefits (total)"                                   # }

title: Get long-run state pension spending projections — get_pension_projections

title: Get long-run state pension spending projections — get_pension_projections
Get long-run state pension spending projections — get_pension_projections
text: refresh Logical. TRUE, re-download even cached copy exists. Defaults FALSE.

title: Get long-run state pension spending projections — get_pension_projections
text: data frame columns: scenario_type Either "Demographic scenarios" "Triple lock scenarios" (character) scenario Scenario name, e.g. "Central projection", "Higher life expectancy" (character) fiscal_year Fiscal year, e.g. "2030-31" (character) pct_gdp State pension spending percentage GDP (numeric)

title: Get long-run state pension spending projections — get_pension_projections
text: data unique Fiscal Risks Sustainability Report (OBR, July 2025) available OBR publication. illustrates ageing demographics pension uprating rules interact determine long-run cost state pension.

title: Get long-run state pension spending projections — get_pension_projections
code: # \donttest{ proj <- get_pension_projections() #> ℹ Downloading fsr_executive_summary.xlsx from OBR... #> ✔ Saved to cache.  # Central demographic projection over the 50-year horizon central <- proj[proj$scenario_type == "Demographic scenarios" &                 proj$scenario == "Central projection", ] tail(central, 10) #>            scenario_type           scenario fiscal_year  pct_gdp #> 42 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2064-65 7.240896 #> 43 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2065-66 7.344158 #> 44 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2066-67 7.448355 #> 45 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2067-68 7.548574 #> 46 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2068-69 7.640862 #> 47 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2069-70 7.732326 #> 48 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2070-71 7.820863 #> 49 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2071-72 7.769048 #> 50 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2072-73 7.661269 #> 51 Demographic scenarios Central projection     2073-74 7.650863  # How much more expensive is 'higher life expectancy' vs central? dem <- proj[proj$scenario_type == "Demographic scenarios", ] # }

title: Get working-age welfare spending — get_welfare_spending
text: Downloads (caches) OBR Welfare Trends Report charts tables workbook returns annual working-age welfare spending share GDP, split incapacity-related non-incapacity spending.

title: Get working-age welfare spending — get_welfare_spending
Get working-age welfare spending — get_welfare_spending
text: refresh Logical. TRUE, re-download even cached copy exists. Defaults FALSE.

title: Get working-age welfare spending — get_welfare_spending
text: data frame columns: year Fiscal year, e.g. "2023-24" (character) series Spending category: "Working-age incapacity benefits spending" "Working-age non-incapacity spending" (character) value Spending percentage GDP (numeric)

title: Get working-age welfare spending — get_welfare_spending
text: Data cover fiscal years 1978-79 current forecast horizon (OBR, October 2024).

title: Get working-age welfare spending — get_welfare_spending

what: List available EFO economy measures — list_efo_economy_measures
title: List available EFO economy measures — list_efo_economy_measures
text: Returns data frame economy measures available via get_efo_economy, showing measure name pass short description covers.

title: List available EFO economy measures — list_efo_economy_measures
List available EFO economy measures — list_efo_economy_measures
text: data frame columns measure, sheet, description.

title: List available EFO economy measures — list_efo_economy_measures
code: list_efo_economy_measures() #>      measure sheet #> 1     labour   1.6 #> 2  inflation   1.7 #> 3 output_gap  1.14 #>                                                                      description #> 1 Labour market: employment, unemployment rate, participation rate, hours worked #> 2                         Inflation: CPI, CPIH, RPI, RPIX, mortgage rates, rents #> 3                 OBR central estimate of the output gap (% of potential output)

title: List available forecast series — list_forecast_series
text: Returns data frame showing series names accepted get_forecasts, corresponding Excel sheet OBR Historical Official Forecasts Database, plain-English description.

title: List available forecast series — list_forecast_series
List available forecast series — list_forecast_series
text: data frame columns series, sheet, description.

title: List available forecast series — list_forecast_series
code: list_forecast_series() #>             series sheet                               description #> 1             PSNB £PSNB         Public sector net borrowing (£bn) #> 2         PSNB_pct  PSNB    Public sector net borrowing (% of GDP) #> 3             PSND  PSND         Public sector net debt (% of GDP) #> 4         receipts £PSCR      Public sector current receipts (£bn) #> 5     receipts_pct  PSCR Public sector current receipts (% of GDP) #> 6      expenditure  £TME           Total managed expenditure (£bn) #> 7  expenditure_pct   TME      Total managed expenditure (% of GDP) #> 8              GDP  NGDP                    Nominal GDP growth (%) #> 9         real_GDP UKGDP                       Real GDP growth (%) #> 10             CPI   CPI                         CPI inflation (%)

title: obr 0.2.2
text: CRAN release: 2026-03-12 Fix README URLs flagged CRAN incoming checks (301 redirects). Add “Databank” WORDLIST.

title: obr 0.2.1
text: Update WORDLIST domain-specific terms (EFO, FSR, WTR, CPIH, etc.)

title: obr 0.2.0
text: get_efo_fiscal() — five-year fiscal projections (net borrowing components) latest Budget get_efo_economy(measure) — quarterly economic projections: "inflation" (CPI, CPIH, RPI, RPIX), "labour" (employment, unemployment, participation), "output_gap" list_efo_economy_measures() — list available economy measures

title: obr 0.2.0
text: get_welfare_spending() — working-age welfare spending split incapacity non-incapacity (% GDP, 1978-79) get_incapacity_spending() — incapacity benefits spending benefit type (ESA, IB, Invalidity Benefit, Sickness Benefit, SDA) % GDP get_incapacity_caseloads() — combined incapacity benefit caseloads prevalence since 2008-09

title: obr 0.2.0
text: get_pension_projections() — 50-year state pension spending projections (% GDP) alternative demographic triple-lock scenarios

title: obr 0.1.0
text: Initial release get_psnb(), get_psnd(), get_expenditure(), get_receipts() Public Finances Databank aggregates get_public_finances() Databank series tidy long format get_forecasts() list_forecast_series() Historical Official Forecasts Database clear_cache() remove locally cached files Data sourced OBR Public Finances Databank Historical Official Forecasts Database
